title: Medicaid Continuous Coverage Requirement and Postpartum Hospitalization.
abstract: In 2020, all US states adopted the Families First Coronavirus Response Act Medicaid continuous coverage requirement (CCR), which prevented disenrollment from Medicaid. This policy effectively extended pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility beyond the previous end date of 60 days post partum.; To determine whether the CCR was associated with a change in postpartum hospitalization rates for mothers covered by Medicaid during their delivery.; This cohort study was conducted from December 2023 to March 2025 at all nonfederal acute care hospitals in 20 states using data from the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project's State Inpatient Databases from 2018 through 2021. Participants included mothers aged 18 to 55 years who had Medicaid-paid deliveries (based on the primary expected payer) from January 2018 to June 2021; they were followed up for 180 days post partum.; State-level postpartum uninsured rate among mothers with Medicaid-paid deliveries that occurred from 2018 to 2019 (before the CCR was implemented in 2020). Residency in states with an uninsured rate above the median was considered to be high exposure (ie, a greater share of the state population had the potential to benefit from the CCR) and residency in states with a preperiod uninsured rate below the median was considered low exposure.; Probability of a mother being hospitalized 1 to 60 days post partum (covered under previous Medicaid eligibility rules) and 61 to 180 days post partum (newly covered under the CCR).; A total of 2 024 214 mothers (mean age, 27.5 [95% CI, 27.5-27.5] years) with Medicaid-paid deliveries were identified. The sample included 550 881 deliveries by Hispanic mothers (27.2%), 490 586 deliveries by non-Hispanic Black mothers (24.2%), 744 945 deliveries by non-Hispanic White mothers (36.8%), 200 639 deliveries by mothers of other races and ethnicities (9.9%), and 37 163 deliveries by mothers with missing race and ethnicity data (1.8%). Among the full sample, 931 452 mothers (mean age, 27.1 [95% CI, 27.71-27.2] years) resided in high-exposure states and 1 092 762 mothers (mean age, 27.8 [95% CI, 27.8-27.8] years) resided in low-exposure states. In adjusted difference-in-differences models, the rate of hospitalization per 1000 Medicaid-paid deliveries at 61 to 180 days post partum decreased (adjusted β coefficient, -1.4; 95% CI, -2.5 to -0.3) in high-exposure states compared with low-exposure states, a 10.9% decrease relative to the preperiod mean (12.9; 95% CI, 12.6 to 13.2) in high-exposure states. The analogous estimate for the hospitalization rates 1 to 60 days post partum was not statistically significant (-0.7 [95% CI, -2.0 to 0.5] per 1000 Medicaid-paid deliveries).; Findings of this study suggest that residency in high-exposure states was associated with a reduction in hospitalizations for mothers 61 to 180 days post partum (the period newly covered by extended Medicaid coverage). Extended Medicaid coverage may help to improve postpartum health and reduce adverse events among mothers with low income.
study_design: Cohort study using difference-in-differences design.
data_years: 2018–2021 inpatient data; deliveries from January 2018 through June 2021 with 180-day postpartum follow-up. Exposure measure used 2018–2019 postpartum uninsurance data.
data_source: Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project State Inpatient Databases from 20 states; PRAMS and ACS used to construct/impute state-level preperiod postpartum uninsurance exposure.
state_or_scope: 20 U.S. states; nonfederal acute-care hospitals
population: Mothers aged 18–55 with Medicaid-paid deliveries from January 2018 through June 2021; N = 2,024,214 Medicaid-paid deliveries
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Families First Coronavirus Response Act Medicaid continuous coverage requirement, which prevented Medicaid disenrollment during the public health emergency and effectively extended pregnancy-related Medicaid beyond 60 days postpartum.
outcomes: Postpartum hospitalization 61–180 days after delivery; placebo hospitalization period 1–60 days postpartum; hospitalization payer type; diagnosis category; subgroup analyses by race/ethnicity and county urbanicity.
equity_focus: Examined differences by race/ethnicity and metropolitan versus nonmetropolitan county. The study also focuses on Medicaid-paid deliveries, a low-income population affected by postpartum coverage loss.
main_findings: After the continuous coverage requirement, high-exposure states had a larger decline in hospitalizations 61–180 days postpartum compared with low-exposure states. Adjusted DID estimate was −1.4 hospitalizations per 1,000 Medicaid-paid deliveries, a 10.9% decline relative to the high-exposure preperiod mean. No significant change was found for the 1–60 day postpartum placebo period. Self-pay postpartum hospitalizations also declined substantially, suggesting fewer uninsured postpartum hospitalizations
limitations: Only 20 states were included, which may limit generalizability. The study could not capture hospitalizations occurring in a different state from delivery or in federal acute-care hospitals. Estimates reflect the COVID-19 pandemic context, so effects may differ outside the pandemic. The authors also caution that the design estimates incremental changes in high- versus low-exposure states rather than a simple causal effect of the policy for all states.
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. Directly relevant to extended postpartum Medicaid coverage and postpartum hospitalization outcomes.

title: COVID-19 Medicaid Continuous Enrollment Provision Yielded Gains In Postpartum Continuity Of Coverage.
abstract: The Medicaid continuous enrollment provision mandated by the Families First Coronavirus Response Act of 2020 effectively prohibited the termination of enrollees from Medicaid during the COVID-19 public health emergency, including people enrolled in Medicaid during pregnancy. Using data from the Transformed Medicaid Statistical Information System, we found that the rate of continuous Medicaid enrollment during the twelve months postpartum increased from 59.3 percent for births during March-December 2018 to 90.7 percent for births during March-December 2020, when the public health emergency was in effect. This corresponds to approximately 430,000 fewer people losing Medicaid coverage after pregnancy and an average of more than 2.5 months of additional postpartum enrollment. These findings indicate that states that have extended or that plan to extend pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility in the postpartum year are likely to experience significant gains in continuity of coverage.
study_design: Descriptive pre-post Medicaid claims analysis.
data_years: 2018–2021 T-MSIS data; compares deliveries from March–December 2018 with deliveries from March–December 2020 and follows Medicaid enrollment for 12 months postpartum.
data_source: Transformed Medicaid Statistical Information System Medicaid claims/enrollment data from CMS.
state_or_scope: All U.S. states; race/ethnicity subgroup analyses excluded states with poor race/ethnicity data quality.
population: Women ages 19–64 with Medicaid-paid deliveries, excluding dual Medicaid/Medicare enrollees. Sample included 1,440,007 unique beneficiaries with deliveries in March–December 2018 and 1,364,240 unique beneficiaries with deliveries in March–December 2020
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous enrollment provision, which prevented Medicaid disenrollment during the COVID-19 public health emergency and functioned as a de facto postpartum Medicaid extension beyond 60 days.
outcomes: Continuous Medicaid enrollment through 12 months postpartum; timing of postpartum Medicaid disenrollment; average postpartum enrollment duration; subgroup differences by eligibility pathway, age, race/ethnicity, Medicaid expansion status, parental eligibility limits, and state.
equity_focus: Race/ethnicity, age, Medicaid eligibility pathway, ACA Medicaid expansion status, state parental income eligibility limits, and immigrant-related coverage pathways such as emergency Medicaid and CHIP unborn child option.
main_findings: Twelve-month continuous postpartum Medicaid enrollment increased from 59.3% among March–December 2018 deliveries to 90.7% among March–December 2020 deliveries. Average postpartum Medicaid enrollment increased from 262 days to 340 days, about 78 additional days. The largest gains occurred among people enrolled through pregnancy Medicaid, people in nonexpansion states, and people in states with low parental Medicaid eligibility limits. The typical third-month postpartum disenrollment cliff was nearly eliminated during continuous enrollment
limitations: The analysis is descriptive and cannot prove causality. Race/ethnicity subgroup results excluded states with poor data quality. The sample did not include pregnancy losses or terminations. The study also relied on Medicaid administrative data, so it could not assess self-reported awareness of coverage or health care utilization outcomes.
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. This is a core coverage-continuity article because it uses national Medicaid administrative data and directly shows how FFCRA continuous enrollment changed postpartum Medicaid retention.

title: Postpartum Medicaid enrollment and churn in North Carolina.
journal: American journal of obstetrics & gynecology MFM
study_design: Retrospective Medicaid enrollment and claims analysis with Cox proportional hazards models for churn after disenrollment.
data_years: January 1, 2016–December 31, 2018.
data_source: North Carolina Medicaid enrollment and claims files for physical health enrollees
state_or_scope: North Carolina, a Medicaid nonexpansion state during the study period.
population: Women ages 12–55 with a Medicaid-insured live birth, at least 30 days of continuous prebirth coverage, and nonemergency Medicaid at birth. Final sample: 55,152 women with Medicaid-covered live births; 13,773 disenrolled after birth
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Pre-2021 Medicaid postpartum eligibility cliff in a nonexpansion state. North Carolina pregnancy Medicaid covered pregnant people up to 201% FPL through 60 days postpartum, but after that they had to qualify through more restrictive eligibility pathways, such as parent/caregiver Medicaid at 41% FPL or aged/blind/disabled Medicaid at 100% FPL.
outcomes: Postpartum Medicaid disenrollment, reenrollment/churn within 6 months, eligibility category transitions, and predictors of churn
equity_focus: Race, age, rural/urban status, county poverty/unemployment/graduation measures, comorbidity, and eligibility category. Black women had higher risk of churn than White women
main_findings: Women with Medicaid-insured births had substantial early postpartum coverage loss. At month 2, only 0.3% were no longer insured by Medicaid, but by month 4, 11.2% were no longer insured. Among women covered by pregnancy Medicaid in month 2, 23.9% lost coverage by month 4. Black women were more likely than White women to churn, and women initially income-qualified for Medicaid were more likely to churn than those in other eligibility categories
limitations: Single-state study in a nonexpansion state, limiting generalizability. The authors did not observe reasons for disenrollment, non-Medicaid insurance coverage, or health care utilization during Medicaid coverage gaps
notes: include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. This is strong evidence of the 60-day postpartum Medicaid cliff, churn, and racial inequity before ARPA/FFCRA-era postpartum coverage changes.

title: Continuous Medicaid Eligibility During the COVID-19 Pandemic and Postpartum Coverage, Health Care, and Outcomes.
abstract: Pursuant to the Families First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA), continuous Medicaid eligibility during the COVID-19 public health emergency (PHE) created a de facto national extension of pregnancy Medicaid eligibility beyond 60 days postpartum.; To evaluate the association of continuous Medicaid eligibility with postpartum health insurance, health care use, breastfeeding, and depressive symptoms.; This cohort study using a generalized difference-in-differences design included 21 states with continuous prepolicy (2017-2019) and postpolicy (2020-2021) participation in the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS).; State-level change in Medicaid income eligibility after 60 days postpartum associated with the FFCRA measured as a percent of the federal poverty level (FPL; ie, the difference in 2020 income eligibility thresholds for pregnant people and low-income adults/parents).; Health insurance, postpartum visit attendance, contraceptive use (any effective method; long-acting reversible contraceptives), any breastfeeding and depressive symptoms at the time of the PRAMS survey (mean [SD], 4 [1.3] months postpartum).; The sample included 47 716 PRAMS respondents (64.4% aged <30 years; 18.9% Hispanic, 26.2% non-Hispanic Black, 36.3% non-Hispanic White, and 18.6% other race or ethnicity) with a Medicaid-paid birth. Based on adjusted estimates, a 100% FPL increase in postpartum Medicaid eligibility was associated with a 5.1 percentage point (pp) increase in reported postpartum Medicaid enrollment, no change in commercial coverage, and a 6.6 pp decline in uninsurance. This represents a 40% reduction in postpartum uninsurance after a Medicaid-paid birth compared with the prepolicy baseline of 16.7%. In subgroup analyses by race and ethnicity, uninsurance reductions were observed only among White and Black non-Hispanic individuals; Hispanic individuals had no change. No policy-associated changes were observed in other outcomes.; In this cohort study, continuous Medicaid eligibility during the COVID-19 PHE was associated with significantly reduced postpartum uninsurance for people with Medicaid-paid births, but was not associated with postpartum visit attendance, contraception use, breastfeeding, or depressive symptoms at approximately 4 months postpartum. These findings, though limited to the context of the COVID-19 PHE, may offer preliminary insight regarding the potential impact of post-pandemic postpartum Medicaid eligibility extensions. Collection of longer-term and more comprehensive follow-up data on postpartum health care and health will be critical to evaluating the effect of ongoing postpartum policy interventions.
study_design: 2017–2021 PRAMS data; prepolicy period 2017–2019 and postpolicy period 2020–2021.
data_years: 2017–2021 PRAMS data; prepolicy period 2017–2019 and postpolicy period 2020–2021.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS), 21 jurisdictions with continuous prepolicy and postpolicy participation.
state_or_scope: 21 jurisdictions: 20 states plus New York City.
population: Postpartum people with Medicaid-paid births; 47,716 PRAMS respondents
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Families First Coronavirus Response Act continuous Medicaid eligibility during the COVID-19 public health emergency, which created a de facto national extension of pregnancy Medicaid beyond 60 days postpartum.
outcomes: Postpartum Medicaid coverage, commercial coverage, uninsurance, postpartum visit attendance, effective contraception, LARC use, breastfeeding, and depressive symptoms around 4 months postpartum.
equity_focus: Race/ethnicity subgroup analyses, including Hispanic, non-Hispanic Black, and non-Hispanic White respondents. The study found uninsurance reductions among Black and White non-Hispanic individuals but not Hispanic individuals.
main_findings: A 100% FPL increase in postpartum Medicaid eligibility was associated with a 5.1 percentage-point increase in postpartum Medicaid enrollment and a 6.6 percentage-point decline in uninsurance, about a 40% reduction from the prepolicy baseline. The policy was not associated with significant changes in postpartum visit attendance, contraception use, breastfeeding, or depressive symptoms.
limitations: Findings may not generalize beyond the 21 jurisdictions. The design depends on parallel-trend assumptions, and some outcomes showed possible prepolicy trend issues. PRAMS captures outcomes relatively early postpartum, around 4 months, so the study may miss later postpartum effects. The study also could not directly assess immigration status or Emergency Medicaid, which may affect interpretation of Hispanic subgroup results.
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. Directly evaluates whether FFCRA continuous Medicaid eligibility reduced postpartum Medicaid loss and uninsurance after Medicaid-paid birth.

title: Rural-Urban Differences in Coverage Gains Under Continuous Postpartum Medicaid Eligibility.
abstract: Rural residents face higher rates of obstetric complications, yet are more likely to lose insurance after childbirth, limiting access to essential postpartum care. This study examined whether continuous Medicaid eligibility during the COVID-19 public health emergency (PHE) differentially impacted postpartum insurance coverage in rural versus urban populations.; Using data from the 2017-2022 American Community Survey, we evaluated how state-level variation in pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility during the PHE influenced coverage among rural and urban residents in the postpartum period.; Medicaid coverage increased significantly for both rural and urban populations. Adjusting for sociodemographic factors and COVID-19 disease burden, rural mothers experienced a 6.4 percentage-point (pp) greater gain in Medicaid coverage per 100-pp increase in the state's pregnancy-related eligibility threshold (95% confidence interval [CI] [1.6, 11.2] pp). Overall postpartum insurance coverage also rose in both groups, although the increase was not significantly larger among rural mothers (3.2-pp greater gain; 95% CI [-0.4, 6.8]).; These findings suggest that recently adopted postpartum Medicaid extensions may yield larger increases in Medicaid coverage in rural communities, an important consideration for policymakers seeking to mitigate the impact of impending Medicaid budget constraints on rural populations.
journal: Women's health issues : official publication of the Jacobs Institute of Women's Health
study_design: Serial cross-sectional study using survey-weighted difference-in-differences models with rural/urban interaction
data_years: 2017–2022 ACS data; pre-policy period 2017–2019; post-policy period 2021–2022.
data_source: American Community Survey Public Use Microdata Sample.
state_or_scope: 43 states including Washington, DC; excluded states with Medicaid expansion during the study period.
population: Postpartum mothers older than 18 who gave birth within the past 12 months and had household income below their state’s pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility threshold; final analytic sample N = 46,575.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA continuous Medicaid eligibility during the COVID-19 public health emergency, which functioned as a national postpartum Medicaid extension by preventing disenrollment after the usual 60-day postpartum period.
outcomes: Self-reported postpartum Medicaid coverage and any health insurance coverage, stratified by rural versus urban residence.
equity_focus: Rural versus urban coverage differences; also adjusted for race/ethnicity, citizenship status, income, education, employment, and other sociodemographic factors.
main_findings: Medicaid coverage increased for both rural and urban postpartum populations, but rural mothers experienced larger gains. Adjusted models showed a 6.4 percentage-point greater gain in Medicaid coverage for rural mothers per 100-percentage-point increase in the state’s pregnancy-related eligibility threshold. Any insurance coverage also increased, but the rural-urban difference was not statistically significant
limitations: ACS does not provide exact timing of survey completion relative to delivery, so some respondents may have been within the first 60 days postpartum and already eligible under prior rules. Insurance coverage was self-reported and may be misclassified, especially because Medicaid is often underreported in surveys. Rural awareness/reporting of coverage may differ. Linear probability models were used despite known limitations.
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. Directly relevant to rural equity and postpartum Medicaid coverage gains under continuous eligibility.

title: Extended Pregnancy Medicaid During COVID-19 and Enrollment and Health Care Use in the Postpartum Year.
abstract: Policy Points The continuous coverage provision of the March 2020 Families First Coronavirus Response Act resulted in extended postpartum Medicaid for individuals with pregnancy Medicaid coverage, which increased postpartum Medicaid enrollment, improved continuity of coverage, and increased Medicaid-paid emergency department visits and mental and behavioral health diagnoses in the 3 to 12 months postpartum. These findings provide insight into the extent to which increased coverage translated into changes in postpartum Medicaid-paid care. Communication and outreach are likely needed to ensure that individuals are aware of and able to use their extended postpartum Medicaid coverage.; Before the COVID-19 pandemic, persons with pregnancy Medicaid coverage were typically disenrolled after 60 days postpartum, at which point they could retain Medicaid only if they qualified through another eligibility category (most commonly as a parent). The March 2020 Families First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) extended postpartum Medicaid coverage by requiring states to pause disenrollment in exchange for enhanced federal funding.; This study examined 2019-2022 Medicaid claims data from 15 states to determine the association between extended postpartum Medicaid coverage and Medicaid-paid care. We employed a continuous difference-in-difference design, leveraging variations in FFCRA-associated eligibility changes (state-level differences in pre-FFCRA pregnancy and parental Medicaid eligibility as a percentage of the federal poverty level [FPL]). The study population included individuals with a birth between January 2019 and December 2021 that was paid for by pregnancy Medicaid coverage. The study population was followed for 12 months after childbirth. Outcomes included Medicaid enrollment, continuity of coverage, outpatient and emergency department visits, and pregnancy-related and mental-health-related diagnoses. Care outcomes were measured from 3 to 12 months postpartum.; In adjusted models, we found that a 100 percentage-point FPL increase in postpartum Medicaid eligibility under the FFCRA was associated with 2.9 additional months of enrollment (95% CI: 0.9, 4.3), a 27.3 percentage-point increase in 12-month continuous Medicaid (95% CI: 2.3, 44.6), 107.2 more emergency department visits per 1,000 beneficiaries (95% CI: 18.7, 167.6), and a 3.2 percentage-point (95% CI: 1.7, 5.4) increase in services with mental and behavioral health diagnoses.; Continuous Medicaid coverage during the FFCRA was associated with longer postpartum enrollment and increases in some health care utilization. However, no increases in Medicaid-paid outpatient care or care for pregnancy-related conditions were found, which may have been due to enrollees' limited awareness of their continued eligibility. Improved communication around extended postpartum Medicaid coverage may improve the translation of coverage into health care access.
study_design: Quasi-experimental cohort study using continuous difference-in-differences design.
data_years: 2019–2022 Medicaid claims data; births from January 2019 through December 2021; individuals followed for 12 months postpartum.
data_source: Transformed Medicaid Statistical Information System Analytic Files (TAF) Medicaid claims data from 15 states; CDC Vital Statistics natality data, unemployment data, and COVID-19 state-month covariates used for adjustment.
state_or_scope: 15 states: Alabama, Georgia, Idaho, Louisiana, Minnesota, North Dakota, Nebraska, Nevada, New York, Oklahoma, Oregon, Rhode Island, South Dakota, Utah, and West Virginia
population: Individuals aged 20–49 with pregnancy Medicaid coverage at delivery; excludes low-income adult/parental Medicaid, emergency Medicaid, and those age 19 or younger. Final sample: 488,601 individuals with pregnancy Medicaid coverage at delivery.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous enrollment provision, which paused disenrollment during the COVID-19 public health emergency and effectively extended postpartum Medicaid beyond 60 days for people with pregnancy Medicaid coverage.
outcomes: Months of postpartum Medicaid enrollment; continuous Medicaid coverage through 12 months postpartum; Medicaid-paid outpatient visits; Medicaid-paid emergency department visits; mental/behavioral health diagnoses; pregnancy-related diagnoses during months 3–12 postpartum.
equity_focus: Limited direct subgroup equity analysis. The study focuses on pregnancy Medicaid enrollees, a low-income postpartum population directly affected by the 60-day coverage cliff. Models include state-level and demographic covariates, including metropolitan residence and birth in the United States.
main_findings: A 100 percentage-point FPL increase in postpartum Medicaid eligibility under FFCRA was associated with 2.9 additional months of Medicaid enrollment, a 27.3 percentage-point increase in continuous Medicaid coverage through 12 months postpartum, 107.2 additional Medicaid-paid ED visits per 1,000 beneficiaries, and a 3.2 percentage-point increase in services with mental/behavioral health diagnoses during months 3–12 postpartum. The main models did not find statistically significant increases in total outpatient visits or pregnancy-related diagnoses
limitations: The data captured only Medicaid-paid health care, so increases may reflect payer shifts rather than total increases in care use. The FFCRA occurred during the COVID-19 pandemic, which may limit generalizability to nonpandemic extensions. The dataset included only 15 states with high-quality TAF data. Some covariates came from state-month vital statistics rather than individual claims records. The continuous difference-in-differences design assumes a linear relationship between eligibility change size and outcomes
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. This is highly relevant because it directly evaluates whether extended postpartum Medicaid coverage translated into enrollment continuity and Medicaid-paid care use during months 3–12 postpartum

title: Extended Postpartum Medicaid Eligibility Is Associated With Improved Continuity Of Coverage In The Postpartum Year.
abstract: The American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 enables states to lengthen eligibility for pregnancy-related Medicaid coverage from the current sixty days after birth to up to one year, a time when mothers remain at elevated pregnancy-related health risk. Using linked birth records, income, and all-payer claims data for Medicaid-paid births in Colorado during the period 2014-19, we compared continuity of coverage during one year postpartum among people eligible for low-income adult Medicaid (with incomes of 138 percent of the federal poverty level or lower) versus those ineligible for Medicaid by any pathway (with incomes of 139 percent of poverty or higher). We found that retention of Medicaid coverage as a low-income adult was associated with 1.5 additional months of postpartum insurance enrollment and a 12-percentage-point increase in the probability of continuous insurance coverage during the first year after birth. Our findings suggest that states that adopt the American Rescue Plan Act option to provide eligibility for pregnancy-related benefits for a full year after birth are likely to improve continuity of postpartum insurance coverage.
study_design: Regression discontinuity study using linked birth records, income data, and all-payer claims.
data_years: 2014–2019 data; Medicaid-paid births from 2014–2018 with one year of postpartum follow-up.
data_source: Colorado All Payer Claims Database, Colorado birth records, and Colorado Medicaid eligibility/income data.
state_or_scope: Colorado.
population: People age 19 or older with Medicaid-financed births in Colorado; final analytic sample included 157,666 births.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Postpartum Medicaid eligibility after 60 days based on Colorado’s low-income adult Medicaid threshold. The study compares people who remained Medicaid-eligible after 60 days postpartum because their income was at or below 138% FPL with those above that threshold who lost Medicaid eligibility after pregnancy-related coverage ended.
outcomes: Months of postpartum insurance enrollment, months of Medicaid enrollment, months of commercial/Marketplace coverage, coverage disruption, coverage switch, coverage gap, APCD coverage loss, and duration of gaps/losses.
equity_focus: Income-based Medicaid eligibility. The study also reports maternal race/ethnicity, education, marital status, birth outside the U.S., chronic conditions, and pregnancy/delivery complications, but the main equity mechanism is income eligibility and the 60-day coverage cliff.
main_findings: Retaining Medicaid eligibility as a low-income adult was associated with 1.54 additional months of any postpartum insurance coverage, 2.01 additional months of Medicaid coverage, and an 11.68 percentage-point reduction in the probability of a coverage disruption during the postpartum year. Descriptively, people ineligible for low-income adult Medicaid after 60 days had much higher disruption rates than those who remained eligible, 43.24% versus 11.92%.
limitations: Findings are from one Medicaid expansion state and may not generalize to nonexpansion states. The regression discontinuity estimates apply most directly to people near the 138% FPL cutoff. The all-payer claims database may miss some self-insured commercial coverage, so some coverage loss may be misclassified. Income was not measured at the exact same point for every person.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. Although published in 2022, the data are pre-ARPA and show why extending postpartum Medicaid eligibility matters: losing eligibility after 60 days was associated with more insurance disruption across the postpartum year.

title: Not all Medicaid for pregnancy care is delivered equally.
abstract: Pregnant beneficiaries in the two primary Medicaid eligibility categories, traditional Medicaid and pregnancy Medicaid, have differing access to care especially in the preconception and postpartum periods. Pregnancy Medicaid has higher income limits for eligibility than traditional Medicaid but only provides coverage during and for a limited time period after pregnancy. Our objective was to determine the association between type of Medicaid (traditional Medicaid and pregnancy Medicaid) on receipt of outpatient care during the perinatal period.; This retrospective cohort study compared outpatient visits using linked birth certificate and Medicaid claims from all Medicaid births in Oregon and South Carolina from 2014 through 2019. Pregnancy Medicaid ended 60 days postpartum during the study. Our primary outcome was average number of outpatient visits per 100 beneficiaries each month during three perinatal time points: preconceputally (three months prior to conception), prenatally (9 months prior to birthdate) and postpartum (from birth to 12 months).; Among 105,808 Medicaid-covered births in Oregon and 141,385 births in South Carolina, pregnancy Medicaid was the most prevelant categorical eligibility. Traditional Medicaid recipients had a higher average number of preconception, prenatal and postpartum visits as compared to those in pregnancy Medicaid.; In South Carolina, those using traditional Medicaid had 450% more preconception visits and 70% more postpartum visits compared with pregnancy Medicaid. In Oregon, those using traditional Medicaid had 200% more preconception visits and 29% more postpartum visits than individuals using pregnancy Medicaid. Lack of coverage in both the preconception and postpartum period deprive women of adequate opportunities to access health care or contraception. Changes to pregnancy Medicaid, including extended postpartum coverage through the American Rescue Plan Act of 2021, may facilitate better continuity of care.
study_design: Retrospective cohort study using linked birth certificate and Medicaid claims data.
data_source: Linked birth certificate and Medicaid claims data from Oregon and South Carolina.
state_or_scope: Oregon and South Carolina.
population: Medicaid-covered live births in Oregon and South Carolina, excluding emergency Medicaid. Sample included 105,808 Medicaid-covered births in Oregon and 141,385 in South Carolina.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Differences between traditional Medicaid and pregnancy Medicaid eligibility. Pregnancy Medicaid had higher income limits but ended 60 days postpartum during the study period, while traditional Medicaid provided ongoing coverage outside pregnancy.
outcomes: Average monthly outpatient visits per 100 beneficiaries during preconception, prenatal, first 60 days postpartum, and 61–365 days postpartum periods; Medicaid enrollment months by perinatal period.
equity_focus: Eligibility category and state policy context. The comparison highlights how people enrolled through pregnancy Medicaid had less continuity and less postpartum care than those enrolled through traditional Medicaid, especially in South Carolina where nonpregnancy Medicaid eligibility was more restrictive.
main_findings: Traditional Medicaid recipients had more preconception, prenatal, and postpartum outpatient visits than pregnancy Medicaid recipients. In South Carolina, traditional Medicaid recipients had 450% more preconception visits and 70% more visits from 61–365 days postpartum than pregnancy Medicaid recipients. In Oregon, traditional Medicaid recipients had 200% more preconception visits and 29% more visits from 61–365 days postpartum. The figure on page 5 visually shows lower outpatient visit intensity for pregnancy Medicaid, especially after 60 days postpartum
limitations: The study used administrative data and could not capture care paid by other insurers or out of pocket. It included only two states, limiting generalizability. Differences between traditional and pregnancy Medicaid enrollees may reflect underlying differences in income, health status, or eligibility pathways. Global maternity billing may undercount prenatal or postpartum visits.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. Strong support for the idea that pregnancy Medicaid’s time-limited eligibility created reduced access to outpatient care before and after pregnancy, especially after the 60-day postpartum cutoff.

title: Factors Associated With Postpartum Uninsurance Among Medicaid-Paid Births.
abstract: To reduce postpartum uninsurance and improve postpartum health, the American Rescue Plan included an option for states to extend pregnancy-related Medicaid from 60 days to 1 year after childbirth. Recent estimates of postpartum uninsurance among Medicaid-paid births would provide information on who would benefit from state adoption of this extension.; To estimate rates of postpartum uninsurance among individuals with Medicaid-paid births.; This cross-sectional study used survey data from the 2015 to 2018 Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS), a representative sample of births in 43 states and New York City, New York, and included PRAMS participants for whom Medicaid was the primary payer for childbirth. The mean weighted PRAMS response rate was 60.9% for the sites and years included in this study. The data were analyzed from December 2020 to January 2021.; Self-reported postpartum uninsurance measured at the time of the PRAMS survey (mean [interquartile range], 4.2 (3.0-5.0) months after birth).; Survey-weighted rates of postpartum uninsurance and 95% CIs overall, by state, by state Medicaid expansion status, and by maternal sociodemographic characteristics. Adjusted odds ratios of the association between maternal characteristics and postpartum uninsurance.; We identified 63 370 respondents with a Medicaid-paid birth. Of these, 22 016 (41.1%) were non-Hispanic White individuals, 17 442 (22.0%) non-Hispanic Black individuals, 6808 (13.3%) Spanish-speaking Hispanic individuals, 7000 (13.7%) English-speaking Hispanic individuals, 2410 (4.0%) Asian/Pacific Islander individuals, and 3894 (1.3%) Indigenous individuals. Of these, 41.7% were no longer insured by Medicaid postpartum and 22.0% were uninsured postpartum. The postpartum uninsurance rate was 3 times higher in Medicaid nonexpansion states (36.8%) compared with expansion states (12.8%). Postpartum uninsurance varied significantly across states, ranging from 1.7% in Massachusetts to 56.7% in Texas. There were substantial racial and ethnic disparities in postpartum uninsurance: 54.9% of Hispanic, Spanish-speaking individuals (adjusted odds ratio [aOR], 6.2; 95% CI, 5.5-7.0) and 42.8% of Indigenous respondents reported postpartum uninsurance (aOR, 4.3; 95% CI, 3.8-4.9) compared with 15.2% of non-Hispanic White respondents. The odds of postpartum uninsurance was higher among unmarried people (aOR, 1.3 compared with married people; 95% CI, 1.2-1.4), those 35 years and older (aOR, 1.5 compared with those younger than 20 years; 95% CI, 1.2-1.9) and those with lower levels of education (aOR, 0.8 for more than high school compared with less than high school; 95% CI, 0.7-0.9).; In this cross-sectional survey study of 43 states, a high proportion of people with Medicaid-paid births were uninsured in the postpartum period, particularly those living in Medicaid nonexpansion states. The study findings suggest that state extensions of pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility through the first year postpartum could disproportionately benefit Hispanic and Indigenous people, unmarried people, those with lower education levels, and those living in Medicaid nonexpansion states.
study_design: Cross-sectional survey study using PRAMS data.
data_years: 2015–2018 PRAMS data.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System from 43 states and New York City
state_or_scope: 43 states and New York City.
population: PRAMS respondents with Medicaid-paid births; final analytic sample included 63,370 individuals.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Pre-2021 postpartum Medicaid coverage loss after Medicaid-paid birth. The study evaluates who became uninsured postpartum under the 60-day pregnancy Medicaid limit and state Medicaid eligibility rules before ARPA postpartum extension implementation.
outcomes: Postpartum uninsurance at the time of the PRAMS survey, measured on average 4.2 months after birth; postpartum insurance category; state-level variation; differences by Medicaid expansion status and sociodemographic characteristics.
equity_focus: State Medicaid expansion status, race/ethnicity, language, Indigenous identity, marital status, education, age, and income. Strong focus on disparities in postpartum uninsurance.
main_findings: Among people with Medicaid-paid births, 41.7% were no longer insured by Medicaid postpartum and 22.0% were uninsured postpartum. Postpartum uninsurance was much higher in Medicaid nonexpansion states than expansion states, 36.8% versus 12.8%. Rates varied widely by state, from 1.7% in Massachusetts to 56.7% in Texas. Spanish-speaking Hispanic respondents and Indigenous respondents had especially high postpartum uninsurance
limitations: Postpartum insurance was self-reported. PRAMS did not include all states and was not nationally representative. The study could not identify immigration status or distinguish emergency Medicaid from pregnancy-related Medicaid. State estimates had varying precision because of differences in response rates and included years.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. This is a core baseline article because it documents the scale and inequity of postpartum uninsurance after Medicaid-paid births before ARPA/FFCRA implementation.

title: Health insurance coverage and poverty status of postpartum women in the United States in 2019: an ACS-PUMS population-based cross-sectional study.
abstract: A quarter of United States (US) postpartum women still report unmet health care needs and health care unaffordability. We aimed to study associations between receipt of health insurance coverage and poverty status/receipt of government financial support and determine coverage gaps overall and by social factors among US postpartum women in poverty.; This study design is a cross-sectional study using secondary data. We included women who gave birth within the last 12 months from 2019 American Community Survey Public Use Microdata Sample. Poverty was defined as having an income-to-poverty ratio of less than 100%. We explored Medicaid/government medical assistance gaps among women in poverty. To examine the associations between Medicaid/government medical assistance (exposures) and poverty/government financial support (outcomes), we used age-, race-, and multivariable-adjusted logistic regression models. We also evaluated the associations of state, race, citizenship status, or language other than English spoken at home (exposures) with receipt of Medicaid/government medical assistance (outcomes) among women in poverty through multivariable-adjusted logistic regression.; It was notable that 35.6% of US postpartum women in poverty did not have Medicaid/government medical assistance and only a small proportion received public assistance income (9.8%)/supplementary security income (3.1%). Women with Medicaid/government medical assistance, compared with those without the coverage, had statistically significantly higher odds of poverty [adjusted odds ratio (aOR): 3.15, 95% confidence interval (95% CI): 2.85-3.48], having public assistance income (aOR: 24.52 [95% CI: 17.31-34.73]), or having supplementary security income (aOR: 4.22 [95% CI: 2.81-6.36]). Also, among postpartum women in poverty, women in states that had not expanded Medicaid, those of Asian or other race, non-US citizens, and those speaking another language had statistically significantly higher odds of not receiving Medicaid/government medical assistance [aORs (95% CIs): 2.93 (2.55-3.37); 1.30 (1.04-1.63); 3.65 (3.05-4.38); and 2.08 (1.86-2.32), respectively].; Our results showed that the receipt of Medicaid/government medical assistance is significantly associated with poverty and having government financial support. However, postpartum women in poverty still had Medicaid/government medical assistance gaps, especially those who lived in states that had not expanded Medicaid, those of Asian or other races, non-US citizens, and other language speakers.
study_design: Population-based cross-sectional study using secondary ACS-PUMS survey data.
data_source: 2019 American Community Survey Public Use Microdata Sample.
state_or_scope: United States, including Washington, DC; Puerto Rico excluded
population: Self-identified women who gave birth within the last 12 months. Study cohort included 34,257 postpartum women, weighted to represent 3,839,270 women. The poverty subgroup represented 802,594 postpartum women, or 20.9% of the weighted sample
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Baseline Medicaid/government medical assistance coverage among postpartum women before ARPA/FFCRA postpartum extension implementation. The study examines Medicaid/public assistance gaps by poverty status, Medicaid expansion status, citizenship, language, and race.
outcomes: Medicaid/government medical assistance coverage, poverty status, public assistance income, supplemental security income, and Medicaid/government assistance coverage gaps among postpartum women in poverty.
equity_focus: Poverty, Medicaid expansion status, race, citizenship status, and language spoken at home. Strong focus on coverage gaps among socially vulnerable postpartum women.
main_findings: Among postpartum women in poverty, 35.6% did not have Medicaid/government medical assistance. Coverage gaps were larger among women in nonexpansion states, non-US citizens, women speaking a language other than English at home, and women categorized as Asian or some other race. In adjusted models, postpartum women in poverty had higher odds of lacking Medicaid/government assistance if they lived in nonexpansion states, were non-US citizens, or spoke another language at home.
limitations: Cross-sectional design, so it cannot evaluate insurance churn or causal effects. The study could not assess the direct impact of uninsurance on maternal or infant outcomes. It also lacked exact birth dates and longitudinal coverage timing, so it could not determine precisely when postpartum coverage gaps occurred.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. It is broader than the Medicaid-paid birth studies, but it is still empirical and directly relevant because it documents pre-extension Medicaid/public insurance coverage gaps among postpartum women in poverty, especially by expansion status, citizenship, language, and race

title: Association of Insurance Changes With Postpartum Prescription Contraception Uptake.
abstract: To assess the association between postpartum insurance churn and postpartum contraception, with specific attention to race, ethnicity, language, and Medicaid expansion status.; We conducted a retrospective cohort study with Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System survey data (2012-2020) from 42 states. Insurance status, assessed more than 2 months after childbirth, was categorized as continuous insurance, insurance loss, discontinuous Medicaid-to-private, discontinuous private-to-Medicaid, continuous uninsurance, and gain of insurance. We used logistic regression to estimate odds ratios with 95% CIs for insurance status and use of prescription contraception methods, compared with nonprescription and no method, and adjusted for age, race and ethnicity, language, marital status, percent of federal poverty level, education, and birth year. We considered the interaction between state Medicaid expansion and insurance status and stratified analyses by race, ethnicity, and language.; Of 223,430 respondents, 78.9% held continuous insurance from childbirth to more than 2 months postpartum and 10.2% lost insurance. Compared with continuous insurance, the adjusted odds ratio (95% CI) for prescription postpartum contraception was 0.74 (0.71-0.78) for insurance loss, 0.90 (0.84-0.97) for discontinuous private-to-Medicaid, and 1.04 (0.98-1.10) for discontinuous Medicaid-to-private. There was no evidence for an interaction between living in states with Medicaid expansion and insurance status. Of respondents without Medicaid expansion, 14.0% (95% CI, 13.6-14.3) experienced insurance loss compared with 6.8% (95% CI, 6.6-7.0) with Medicaid expansion. There was a significant interaction between insurance discontinuity and race, ethnicity, and language. Nearly half of the Hispanic Spanish-language respondents (47.5%) experienced insurance loss postpartum.; Insurance loss more than 2 months postpartum was associated with decreased odds of prescription contraception. Populations that experience higher rates of insurance loss include respondents in states without Medicaid expansion and Hispanic Spanish-language respondents. Medicaid and insurance policies have important roles in enabling coverage for contraceptive access across racial and ethnic groups during the postpartum period.
study_design: Retrospective cohort study using PRAMS survey data.
data_years: 2012–2020 PRAMS data.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System from 42 states.
state_or_scope: 42 U.S. states.
population: Postpartum PRAMS respondents who completed the survey more than 2 months and up to 1 year after birth, with complete insurance, contraception, and covariate data. Final analytic sample: 223,430 respondents
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Postpartum insurance churn after childbirth, including loss of insurance after pregnancy-related Medicaid coverage and differences by Medicaid expansion status. The study connects insurance continuity/loss to postpartum prescription contraception access.
outcomes: Use of prescription postpartum contraception versus nonprescription or no method. Prescription methods included pills, patch/ring, injection, diaphragm, IUD, implant, sterilization, and vasectomy.
equity_focus: Race, ethnicity, language, and Medicaid expansion status. The study specifically examines Hispanic Spanish-language respondents, who had very high postpartum insurance loss.
main_findings: Most respondents had continuous insurance, but 10.2% lost insurance postpartum. Insurance loss was associated with lower odds of prescription contraception compared with continuous insurance, with an adjusted OR of 0.74. Respondents in nonexpansion states had higher insurance loss than those in expansion states, 14.0% vs 6.8%. Nearly half of Hispanic Spanish-language respondents, 47.5%, experienced postpartum insurance loss
limitations: PRAMS excluded several states, which may limit generalizability. The study could not examine insurance changes within the same insurance category, such as Medicaid managed care changes. It could not directly identify Emergency Medicaid or immigration status. Missing income data may introduce bias. The study also did not include community or patient engagement in research design.
notes: Include as service-specific Medicaid access policy or, if you want fewer categories, pre-2021 baseline problem evidence with service-access outcome. This is strong empirical evidence that postpartum insurance loss/churn is associated with lower prescription contraception use, and that insurance loss is concentrated among nonexpansion states and Hispanic Spanish-language respondents

title: Changes in postpartum insurance and care use by disability status during the COVID-19 pandemic.
abstract: People with disabilities face unique health needs and barriers to perinatal care. The pandemic may have worsened health care access disparities, while pandemic-era Medicaid provisions potentially improved access via increased insurance coverage.; We assessed changes in postpartum insurance, visits, and reproductive health care during the COVID-19 public health emergency (PHE) and PHE Medicaid provisions among individuals with disabilities versus individuals without disabilities.; We used the 2019-2020 Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System survey and Disability Supplement to compare changes in postpartum outcomes by disability status during COVID-19. Adjusted regression models included an interaction term between disability status and postpartum exposure to the PHE. Comparative differences were examined overall, among low-income respondents, and among respondents with Medicaid-paid deliveries.; During the PHE, there was a significant increase in postpartum Medicaid by 7.1% points (95 % CI: 0.6, 13.6) and a decrease in uninsurance by 5.2% points (95 % CI: -9.0, -1.4) among respondents with disabilities relative to those without. There was a significant increase in postpartum contraception during the PHE among respondents with disabilities relative to those without by 6.3% points (95 % CI: -0.1, 12.5). The PHE was associated with larger increases in postpartum Medicaid and larger decreases in postpartum uninsurance among low-income respondents, with similar estimates among respondents with Medicaid-insured deliveries.; During the COVID-19 PHE, individuals with disabilities saw increased postpartum insurance and improved contraceptive use. As PHE Medicaid provisions are rolled back, these differential improvements should be factored into decisions about postpartum Medicaid eligibility.
study_design: Cross-sectional PRAMS survey study using difference-in-differences models comparing changes during the COVID-19 PHE by disability status.
data_years: 2019–2020 PRAMS data.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System survey and PRAMS Disability Supplement with Washington Group Short Set disability measures.
state_or_scope: 17 states and Washington, DC.
population: Postpartum PRAMS respondents with recent live births; weighted sample of 1,530,746 postpartum individuals, including 98,547 individuals with disabilities.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: COVID-19 PHE/FFCRA Medicaid continuous coverage provisions, which reduced postpartum Medicaid loss after pregnancy-related coverage and increased postpartum Medicaid retention.
outcomes: Postpartum Medicaid coverage, private/military coverage, uninsurance, postpartum check-up attendance, any postpartum contraception, short-acting contraception, long-acting contraception, and permanent contraception.
equity_focus: Disability status. The study compares postpartum insurance and care-use changes among individuals with disabilities versus those without disabilities. It also examines low-income respondents and respondents with Medicaid-paid deliveries.
main_findings: During the PHE, respondents with disabilities had a larger increase in postpartum Medicaid coverage and larger decrease in uninsurance compared with respondents without disabilities. Adjusted models showed postpartum Medicaid increased by 7.1 percentage points and uninsurance decreased by 5.2 percentage points among respondents with disabilities relative to those without. Any postpartum contraception also increased among respondents with disabilities, but postpartum check-up use did not significantly increase.
limitations: Limited to 17 states and DC that used the disability supplement and met PRAMS response thresholds. Only 2019–2020 data were available, so longer pre-trends could not be assessed. Outcomes were self-reported and may include recall or measurement error. Sample size limited subgroup analyses by disability type and race/ethnicity
notes: Relevant post-2021/PHE policy evidence focused on disability equity. It does not directly evaluate ARPA 12-month extension, but it provides empirical evidence that pandemic-era Medicaid continuous coverage was associated with improved postpartum Medicaid coverage and reduced uninsurance for people with disabilities

title: Postpartum Insurance Loss: Predicting Factors, Associations with Postpartum Health Service Utilization, and the Role of Medicaid Expansion.
abstract: This study investigated the predictors of postpartum insurance loss (PPIL), assessed its association with postpartum healthcare receipt, and explored the potential buffering role of Medicaid expansion.; Data from the 2016-2020 Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS) were analyzed, covering 197,820 individuals with live births. PPIL was determined via self-reported insurance status before and after pregnancy. Postpartum visits and depression screening served as key health service receipt indicators. The association between PPIL and maternal characteristics was examined using bivariate analysis. The association of PPIL with health service receipt was assessed through odds ratios derived from multivariate logistic regression models. The role of Medicaid expansion was explored by interacting ACA Medicaid expansion status with the dichotomous PPIL indicator.; PPIL was experienced by 7.8% of postpartum people, with higher rates in Medicaid non-expansion states (13.6%) compared to 6.1% in expansion states (p < 0.05). Racial and ethnic disparities were observed, with 16.5% of Hispanic and 4.6% of white people experiencing PPIL. Individuals who experienced PPIL had decreased odds of attending postpartum visits (adjusted odds ratio (aOR) = 0.81, 95% CI = 0.73-0.90) and receiving screening for postpartum depression (aOR = 0.86, 95% CI = 0.78-0.96) compared to those who maintained insurance coverage. People in expansion states with no PPIL had higher odds of postpartum depression screening (aOR = 1.33, 95% CI = 1.08-1.62). No differences in postpartum visits in expansion versus non-expansion were noted (aOR = 1.13, 95% CI = 0.93-1.36).; Ensuring consistent postpartum insurance coverage offers policymakers a chance to enhance healthcare access and outcomes, particularly for vulnerable groups.
study_design: Cross-sectional study using PRAMS survey data with multivariable logistic regression.
data_years: 2016–2020 PRAMS data; sensitivity analysis excluded 2020.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System.
state_or_scope: 45 states and Washington, DC represented in PRAMS, though state inclusion varied by year based on PRAMS response-rate thresholds.
population: Postpartum individuals with live births; final analytic sample included 197,820 individuals after exclusions
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Postpartum insurance loss after pregnancy, shaped by Medicaid eligibility limits and ACA Medicaid expansion status. The study examines whether Medicaid expansion buffered insurance loss and postpartum service access
outcomes: Postpartum insurance loss, postpartum visit attendance, and postpartum depression screening.
equity_focus: Medicaid expansion status, race/ethnicity, income, education, prenatal Medicaid coverage, and Hispanic ethnicity. The study highlights higher postpartum insurance loss in Medicaid nonexpansion states and among Hispanic postpartum people
main_findings: Overall, 7.8% of postpartum people experienced insurance loss after delivery. Insurance loss was higher in Medicaid nonexpansion states than expansion states, 13.6% vs 6.1%. Hispanic postpartum people had the highest insurance-loss rate at 16.5%. People who lost insurance had lower odds of attending a postpartum visit and lower odds of receiving postpartum depression screening
limitations: nsurance status was self-reported and may be misclassified. PRAMS captures insurance status around 2–6 months postpartum, so later insurance loss may be missed. Results may not generalize to states not included in PRAMS or years that did not meet response thresholds. The study shows associations, not causality.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. It is useful because it connects postpartum insurance loss to actual service use: postpartum visits and depression screening. It also supports the argument that Medicaid expansion and postpartum coverage continuity matter for access to care.

title: Pregnancy Medicaid Improvements in a Nonexpansion State After the Affordable Care Act.
abstract: One-half of women in the United States use Medicaid during pregnancy. Women living in states that did not expand Medicaid under the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (ACA) are at risk of losing coverage post partum. We analyzed Medicaid claims and vital statistics for the state of North Carolina for the period 2011 to 2017. North Carolina did not expand Medicaid but did alter Medicaid enrollment to meet ACA requirements. After implementation, enrollment in full Medicaid during pregnancy almost doubled, and enrollment in Medicaid for pregnant women decreased. Full Medicaid offers more comprehensive coverage and does not expire at 60 days post partum, allowing for access to crucial preventive health services including contraception and primary care.
study_design: Descriptive pre-post administrative data study using Medicaid claims and vital statistics
data_years: 2011–2017 North Carolina Medicaid claims and vital statistics data
data_source: North Carolina Medicaid claims and vital statistics data.
state_or_scope: North Carolina, a Medicaid nonexpansion state.
population: Women younger than 50 enrolled in Medicaid with at least two Medicaid-covered births between 2011 and 2017. Final sample included 172,208 pregnancies among 82,997 women
medicaid_policy_mechanism: ACA-mandated Medicaid enrollment process changes in North Carolina, including electronic enrollment and modified adjusted gross income rules. The study examines shifts between Medicaid for pregnant women, which ended 60 days postpartum, and full Medicaid, which did not expire postpartum
outcomes: Monthly Medicaid-covered births by coverage type: Medicaid for pregnant women, full Medicaid, and emergency Medicaid.
equity_focus: Coverage category and postpartum coverage continuity in a nonexpansion state. The article is relevant because coverage type determined whether postpartum coverage ended after 60 days or continued.
main_findings: After ACA-related enrollment reforms, enrollment in full Medicaid during pregnancy increased from 32% to 58% of pregnancies, while enrollment in Medicaid for pregnant women decreased from 55% to 35%. The mean number of births per month covered by full Medicaid nearly doubled, from 661 before reform to 1,192 after reform
limitations: Single-state study, so generalizability is limited. The analysis used administrative data and did not include detailed financial eligibility information. It also focused on coverage type changes, not direct postpartum health outcomes or care utilization.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. It is not about ARPA, but it is useful background evidence showing that Medicaid eligibility category matters because full Medicaid continued postpartum while pregnancy Medicaid ended after 60 days. Data are not entirely pre-2015, so it should not be excluded under the pre-2015 cohort rule.

title: Association of COVID-19 Continuous Enrollment With Self-Reported Postpartum Medicaid Continuity and Coverage Inequities.
abstract: To examine the impact of extended postpartum Medicaid eligibility under the Families First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) on self-reported postpartum insurance status among prenatal Medicaid recipients, and differences by state Medicaid expansion status and race, and ethnicity.; We used a global polynomial linear regression discontinuity design (RDD) approach to estimate the effect of extended postpartum Medicaid eligibility during the FFCRA on changes in self-reported postpartum Medicaid, private coverage, and uninsurance. This approach compares individuals who gave birth before FFCRA exposure with those who gave birth during extended postpartum Medicaid eligibility, using birth timing to determine FFCRA exposure. We estimated RDD models overall, by state Medicaid expansion status, and by race and ethnicity.; This study used 2018-2021 Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System data, a multi-state survey of individuals with a recent live birth, and a sample of prenatal Medicaid recipients age 20 or older in 29 study jurisdictions.; In adjusted RDD models, extended Medicaid eligibility was associated with a 10.7 percentage point (pp) (95% CI: 8.7, 12.6) increase in postpartum Medicaid, a 3.5 pp (95% CI: -5.2, -1.8) decrease in postpartum private coverage, and a 6.5 pp (95% CI: -8.0, -5.0) decrease in postpartum uninsurance. In stratified RDD models, we found larger increases in postpartum Medicaid and larger decreases in uninsurance in non-expansion states than in Medicaid expansion states. In RDD models by race and ethnicity, we found similar increases in postpartum Medicaid and similar decreases in postpartum uninsurance among non-Hispanic Black respondents, Hispanic respondents, and non-Hispanic White respondents.; We found significant improvements in postpartum Medicaid continuity and reductions in uninsurance during extended postpartum Medicaid eligibility. Postpartum Medicaid extensions under the American Rescue Plan could help maintain some coverage gains under the FFCRA.
study_design: Regression discontinuity design using PRAMS survey data.
data_years: 2018–2021 PRAMS data.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System from 29 jurisdictions.
state_or_scope: 29 jurisdictions with PRAMS data meeting response criteria.
population: Prenatal Medicaid recipients age 20 or older with a recent live birth; 46,737 respondents, weighted N = 2,148,676 postpartum individuals.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous enrollment provision, which extended postpartum Medicaid eligibility beyond 60 days during the COVID-19 public health emergency.
outcomes: Self-reported postpartum Medicaid coverage, private coverage, dual Medicaid/private coverage, and uninsurance at about 2–6 months postpartum.
equity_focus: State Medicaid expansion status and race/ethnicity. The study compares expansion vs nonexpansion states and stratifies by non-Hispanic Black, Hispanic, and non-Hispanic White respondents.
main_findings: Extended Medicaid eligibility was associated with a 10.7 percentage-point increase in postpartum Medicaid, a 3.5 percentage-point decrease in private coverage, and a 6.5 percentage-point decrease in uninsurance. Effects were larger in nonexpansion states: Medicaid increased 15.5 percentage points and uninsurance decreased 10.6 percentage points. In race/ethnicity models, Medicaid coverage increased and uninsurance decreased for Black, Hispanic, and White respondents, but Hispanic respondents still had higher persistent uninsurance
limitations: The FFCRA policy occurred at the same time as the pandemic, making it difficult to fully separate policy effects from pandemic effects. Insurance status was self-reported, so Medicaid may be underreported. PRAMS captures early postpartum coverage, usually 2–6 months after birth, not the full postpartum year. The 29 jurisdictions may not be nationally generalizable, and the study had limited power for some racial/ethnic groups
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. Useful for coverage equity because it directly examines postpartum Medicaid continuity, uninsurance reductions, Medicaid expansion status, and racial/ethnic differences under FFCRA continuous enrollment

title: Association of Medicaid Expansion Under the Affordable Care Act With Medicaid Coverage in the Prepregnancy, Prenatal, and Postpartum Periods.
abstract: We evaluated how the Affordable Care Act (ACA) Medicaid eligibility expansion affected perinatal insurance coverage patterns for Medicaid-enrolled beneficiaries who gave birth overall and by race/ethnicity. We also examined state-level heterogeneous impacts.; Using the 2011-2013 Medicaid Analytic eXtract and the 2016-2018 Transformed Medicaid Statistical Information System Analytic File databases, we identified 1.4 million beneficiaries giving birth in 2012 (pre-ACA expansion cohort) and 1.5 million in 2017 (post-ACA expansion cohort). We constructed monthly coverage rates for the two cohorts by state Medicaid expansion status and obtained difference-in-differences estimates of the association of Medicaid expansion with coverage overall and by race/ethnicity group (non-Hispanic White, non-Hispanic Black, and Hispanic). To explore state-level heterogeneous impacts, we divided the expansion and non-expansion states into groups based on the differences in the income eligibility limits for low-income parents in each state between 2012 and 2017.; Medicaid expansion was associated with 13 percentage points higher coverage in the 9 to 12 months before giving birth, and 11 percentage points higher coverage at 6 to 12 months postpartum. Hispanic birthing individuals had the greatest relative increases in coverage, followed by non-Hispanic White and non-Hispanic Black individuals. In Medicaid expansion states, those who experienced the greatest increases in income eligibility limits for low-income parents generally saw the greatest increases in coverage. In non-expansion states, there was less heterogeneity between state groupings.; Pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility did not have major changes in the 2010s. However, states' adoption of ACA Medicaid expansion after 2012 was associated with increased Medicaid coverage before, during, and after pregnancy. The increases varied by race/ethnicity and across states.
journal: Women's health issues : official publication of the Jacobs Institute of Women's Health
study_design: Difference-in-differences study using Medicaid administrative claims/enrollment data.
data_years: 2011–2013 MAX data and 2016–2018 T-MSIS Analytic File data; compares people with Medicaid-covered live births in 2012 and 2017.
data_source: Medicaid Analytic eXtract and Transformed Medicaid Statistical Information System Analytic Files.
state_or_scope: All U.S. states and Washington, DC, excluding Louisiana from the main analysis because its Medicaid expansion timing did not allow full 12-month prebirth observation.
population: Medicaid beneficiaries aged 18–44 with a Medicaid-insured live birth. Sample included about 1.4 million beneficiaries giving birth in 2012 and 1.5 million giving birth in 2017.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: ACA Medicaid expansion for low-income adults/parents. The policy mechanism is broader Medicaid eligibility outside pregnancy, which can improve coverage before pregnancy and after the 60-day postpartum pregnancy Medicaid limit.
outcomes: Monthly Medicaid enrollment during the 12 months before and 12 months after childbirth; coverage differences by ACA expansion status, race/ethnicity, state, and changes in parent income eligibility limits.
equity_focus: Race/ethnicity and state Medicaid expansion status. The study separately examines non-Hispanic White, non-Hispanic Black, and Hispanic birthing individuals.
main_findings: ACA Medicaid expansion was associated with higher Medicaid coverage before and after childbirth: about 13 percentage points higher coverage 9–12 months before birth and about 11 percentage points higher coverage 6–12 months postpartum. Hispanic birthing individuals experienced the largest relative increases in Medicaid coverage, followed by non-Hispanic White and non-Hispanic Black individuals
limitations: The study only observes Medicaid coverage, not other insurance sources, so total insurance coverage may be underestimated. It uses two different Medicaid data systems across periods, which may introduce data-quality differences. Race/ethnicity data quality varied by state, and some states were excluded from stratified analyses. The analysis focuses on coverage, not direct postpartum health outcomes.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. It is not a 12-month postpartum extension study, but it is directly relevant because it shows that broader Medicaid eligibility outside pregnancy improves postpartum coverage continuity after the 60-day pregnancy Medicaid limit.

title: Association of Continuous Medicaid Eligibility With Postpartum Coverage and Opioid Use Disorder Treatment.
abstract: Over three quarters of pregnant people with opioid use disorder are enrolled in Medicaid at delivery, but pregnancy-related Medicaid eligibility has historically ended 60 days postpartum, leading to periods without insurance and decreased access to health care during the first year postpartum. The Families First Coronavirus Response Act, passed in March 2020, prevented states from disenrolling Medicaid enrollees, including postpartum enrollees, during the COVID-19 Public Health Emergency.; A difference-in-difference model was used to compare the continuity of coverage among postpartum persons with opioid use disorder in Medicaid versus commercial insurance before and after the Families First Coronavirus Response Act . A segmented regression model was used to assess medication for opioid use disorder retention (at 6 and 12 months) among those with Medicaid at delivery before (2016-2019) versus after Families First Coronavirus Response Act (2020-2022).; Among deliveries from 2016 to 2022, 1,435 (10.2%) were enrolled in commercial insurance, and 12,567 (89.8%) were in Medicaid. In the pre-Families First Coronavirus Response Act period, 60.0% of commercially insured and 63.5% of Medicaid-insured deliveries remained enrolled through 12-months postpartum. In adjusted analyses, the Families First Coronavirus Response Act was associated with a 23.2 percentage-point (95% CI=17.6%, 28.8%) increase in 12-month continuous Medicaid coverage compared with that among commercially insured individuals. Among Medicaid enrollees, medication for opioid use disorder retention increased by 14.4 (95% CI=8.2, 20.6) and 16.3 (95% CI=10.1, 22.5) percentage points (level change) in 6- and 12-month medication for opioid use disorder retention, respectively, after the Families First Coronavirus Response Act.; In this observational study, the Families First Coronavirus Response Act was associated with increased health insurance stability and improved medication for opioid use disorder retention among postpartum Medicaid beneficiaries with opioid use disorder. These findings suggest that postpartum Medicaid extensions, implemented in 49 states and Washington, DC, could help increase insurance coverage stability and medication for opioid use disorder use for this high-risk population.
study_design: Observational study using difference-in-differences for continuous postpartum coverage and segmented regression for MOUD retention.
data_years: 2016–2022 deliveries, excluding 2019 as a washout year for primary models.
data_source: TriNetX Linked electronic medical records and linked insurance claims from 29 health care organizations and 150 insurance plans.
state_or_scope: National multi-organization U.S. sample; state identifiers unavailable. Medicaid expansion status categorized as nonexpansion, expansion by 2014, or expansion after 2014.
population: Postpartum people aged 18–44 with opioid use disorder who delivered between 2016 and 2022. Sample included 14,002 deliveries: 12,567 Medicaid-insured and 1,435 commercially insured at delivery.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous enrollment provision, which prevented Medicaid disenrollment during the COVID-19 public health emergency and extended postpartum coverage stability for Medicaid enrollees beyond the usual 60-day pregnancy-related eligibility period.
outcomes: Twelve-month continuous postpartum insurance coverage; 6-month and 12-month postpartum medication for opioid use disorder retention among Medicaid enrollees with MOUD at delivery.
equity_focus: Focuses on postpartum people with OUD, a high-risk population with substantial Medicaid enrollment. Also examines differences by Medicaid expansion status.
main_findings: FFCRA was associated with a 23.2 percentage-point increase in 12-month continuous Medicaid coverage compared with commercially insured individuals. Gains were largest in nonexpansion states. Among Medicaid enrollees with MOUD at delivery, MOUD retention increased by 14.4 percentage points at 6 months and 16.3 percentage points at 12 months after FFCRA.
limitations: TriNetX data may not generalize to all postpartum people with OUD. Care outside linked organizations or unlinked claims may be missed. State identifiers were unavailable, so state policy context could only be approximated by Medicaid expansion status. Sample size was insufficient to examine MOUD retention by expansion status. Other pandemic-era changes, especially telehealth and relaxed MOUD prescribing rules, may also have affected retention
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation evidence and service-relevant evidence. Directly links Medicaid continuous coverage to both insurance stability and postpartum OUD treatment retention.

title: Effects of Medicaid Coverage on Work: Evidence From Extending Postpartum Medicaid Coverage.
abstract: To evaluate the effects of the Family First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) on work outcomes of women for whom the FFCRA effectively expanded income eligibility for Medicaid beyond 60 days postpartum by prohibiting states from redetermining Medicaid eligibility between March 2020 and March 2023.; We use a difference-in-differences design that leverages the differences in income eligibility between pregnancy and non-pregnancy across states, and compares outcome changes pre-post FFCRA over these differences.; Data come from the 2016-2022 American Community Survey. The sample includes 205,104 women aged 19-49 years who reported giving birth within the past 12 months in 41 states and Washington D.C.; On average, the FFCRA increased postpartum Medicaid coverage by 2.8 percentage points (95% CI: 0.7-4.8) or by 9.3% relative to the 2019 Medicaid coverage rate. In contrast, the FFCRA effects on work outcomes were small and not significant: the average effect was 0.10 percentage points for labor force participation (95% CI: -1.0 to 1.2), 0.7 percentage points for employment (95% CI: -0.02 to 1.4), 0.04 h for weekly work hours (95% CI: -0.4 to 0.5), and 0.2 percentage points for full-time employment (95% CI: -1.1 to 1.5). These confidence intervals rule out an employment decline above 0.02 percentage points and full-time employment decline above 1.1 percentage points. The increase in Medicaid coverage is concentrated among states with a larger difference between pregnancy and non-pregnancy eligibility (+5.9 percentage points; 95% CI: 0.9 to 10.9) and estimates in this group also rule out relatively small declines in work outcomes.; There is no evidence of declines in work outcomes following the increase in Medicaid coverage beyond 60 days postpartum that resulted from the FFCRA. The findings suggest that subsequent postpartum Medicaid coverage extensions for 12 months under the American Rescue Plan are unlikely to disincentivize work among beneficiaries.
study_design: Difference-in-differences study using repeated cross-sectional survey data
data_years: 2016–2022 ACS data; pre-FFCRA period 2016–2019 and FFCRA period 2020–2022.
data_source: American Community Survey.
state_or_scope: 41 states and Washington, DC; excludes states that expanded Medicaid during the study period to avoid confounding.
population: Women aged 19–49 who reported giving birth within the past 12 months; final sample N = 205,104.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous coverage provision, which effectively extended postpartum Medicaid coverage beyond 60 days by preventing eligibility redetermination/disenrollment during March 2020–March 2023.
outcomes: Postpartum Medicaid coverage; labor force participation; employment status; weekly work hours; full-time employment.
equity_focus: Subgroup analyses by income, education, age, race/ethnicity, marital status, and states with larger versus smaller pregnancy/nonpregnancy Medicaid eligibility gaps.
main_findings: FFCRA increased postpartum Medicaid coverage by 2.8 percentage points overall, a 9.3% increase relative to the 2019 Medicaid coverage rate. Effects on work outcomes were small and not statistically significant, with no evidence of declines in labor force participation, employment, work hours, or full-time employment. The Medicaid coverage increase was concentrated in states with larger pregnancy versus nonpregnancy eligibility gaps
limitations: ACS identifies whether someone gave birth in the past 12 months but not the exact postpartum month, so timing within the postpartum year is unclear. The FFCRA occurred during the COVID-19 pandemic, which also affected labor markets. Medicaid coverage may be underreported in survey data. Findings may not generalize to other Medicaid eligibility changes or non-postpartum adult populations.
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. This is not a health care utilization outcome study, but it directly evaluates a policy concern tied to postpartum Medicaid extension: whether extended coverage affects work.

title: Immigrant Inequities in Uninsurance and Postpartum Medicaid Extension: A Quasi-Experimental Study in New York City, 2016-2021.
abstract: Objectives. To determine if de facto postpartum Medicaid extension during the Families First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) reduced immigrant versus US-born inequities in uninsurance. Methods.
