We assessed self-reported uninsurance at 2 to 6 months postpartum among people with Medicaid-paid births using the New York City Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS), comparing immigrant and US-born people. We created a pre-FFCRA cohort of 2611 births from 2016 to 2019 and a post-FFCRA implementation cohort of 1197 births from 2020 to 2021. We calculated risk differences using log binomial regression. Results. Self-reported postpartum uninsurance among immigrants decreased from 13.6% to 9.3% after FFCRA (adjusted risk difference = -4.9%; 95% confidence interval = -7.8%, -2.0%). Immigrant versus US-born inequities in postpartum uninsurance decreased except among Hispanic birthing people, among whom 1 in 6 reported they were uninsured during FFCRA, despite continued eligibility. Conclusions. De facto postpartum Medicaid extension decreased immigrant inequities in insurance coverage, but Hispanic immigrants may have been unaware of continued coverage. Public Health Implications. Postpartum Medicaid extension policies that are inclusive of all immigrants may decrease inequities, but community-integrated implementation is needed to raise awareness of coverage and advance postpartum maternal health equity. (Am J Public Health. 2025;115(5):732-735. https://doi.org/10.2105/AJPH.2024.307968).
study_design: Quasi-experimental study using difference-in-differences models
data_years: 2016–2021 NYC PRAMS data; pre-FFCRA births from 2016–2019; post-FFCRA implementation births from 2020–2021, with a washout period around implementation.
data_source: New York City Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System linked with birth certificate data.
state_or_scope: NYC
population: People with Medicaid-paid births in NYC, comparing immigrant and US-born postpartum respondents; pre-FFCRA cohort n = 2,611 and post-FFCRA cohort n = 1,197.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous enrollment/maintenance-of-eligibility provision, which created a de facto postpartum Medicaid extension in New York and prevented disenrollment after birth regardless of immigration status.
outcomes: Self-reported postpartum uninsurance at 2–6 months postpartum.
equity_focus: Immigrant versus US-born inequities, with subgroup attention to race/ethnicity, especially Hispanic immigrants.
main_findings: Postpartum uninsurance among immigrants decreased from 13.6% before FFCRA to 9.3% after FFCRA, with an adjusted risk difference of −4.9 percentage points. Immigrant versus US-born inequities in uninsurance decreased overall, but Hispanic immigrants remained at high risk: about 1 in 6 reported being uninsured during FFCRA despite continued eligibility
limitations: Postpartum insurance was self-reported. The study did not have legal immigration status, which limits interpretation of which immigrant groups were expected to gain coverage. The study is limited to NYC and may not generalize to states that exclude some immigrant groups from postpartum coverage.
notes: Strong post-2021 policy/implementation and equity evidence. This is especially useful for the review’s equity theme because it directly evaluates whether de facto postpartum Medicaid extension reduced immigrant-related postpartum uninsurance inequities.

title: Medicaid Expansion Improved Perinatal Insurance Continuity For Low-Income Women.
abstract: Insurance churn, or moving between different insurance plans or between insurance and uninsurance, is common during the perinatal period. We used survey data from the 2012-17 Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System to estimate the impact of Affordable Care Act-related state Medicaid expansions on continuity of insurance coverage for low-income women across three time points: preconception, delivery, and postpartum. We found that Medicaid expansion resulted in a 10.1-percentage-point decrease in churning between insurance and uninsurance, representing a 28 percent decrease from the prepolicy baseline in expansion states. This decrease was driven by a 5.8-percentage-point increase in the proportion of women who were continuously insured and a 4.2-percentage-point increase in churning between Medicaid and private insurance. Medicaid expansion improved insurance continuity in the perinatal period for low-income women, which may improve the quality of perinatal health care, but it also increased churning between public and private health insurance.
study_design: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences study using PRAMS survey data.
data_years: 2012–2017 PRAMS data.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System from 19 states and New York City.
state_or_scope: 14 Medicaid expansion jurisdictions and 6 nonexpansion jurisdictions with continuous PRAMS participation during the study period.
population: Low-income women with household incomes below 138% FPL who had a live birth and complete insurance information. Final sample included 47,617 women, representing about 2.1 million low-income women.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: ACA Medicaid expansion, which expanded Medicaid eligibility for low-income adults independent of pregnancy status and reduced perinatal insurance churn before, during, and after pregnancy.
outcomes: Perinatal insurance continuity across preconception, delivery, and postpartum periods; continuous insurance; Medicaid–private churn; insured–uninsured churn; continuous uninsurance.
equity_focus: Low-income postpartum population, Medicaid expansion status, and implications for Black and Hispanic women, who are more likely to experience perinatal insurance instability and live in nonexpansion states.
main_findings: Medicaid expansion was associated with a 10.1 percentage-point decline in insured–uninsured churn, a 5.8 percentage-point increase in continuous insurance, and a 4.2 percentage-point increase in Medicaid–private churn. The authors interpret this as a meaningful improvement in perinatal insurance continuity, though discontinuity remained common even after expansion
limitations: The study included 19 states and New York City, so results may not generalize to all states. PRAMS insurance measures are partly self-reported. The study could not capture churn within Medicaid plans or within private insurance plans, so insurance disruption may be underestimated. Only two prepolicy years were available, limiting assessment of parallel trends.
notes: nclude as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. It is not a postpartum extension study, but it directly supports the baseline argument that broader Medicaid eligibility outside pregnancy improved perinatal insurance continuity and reduced churn before ARPA/FFCRA-era 12-month postpartum extensions.

title: Impact of 12-month Medicaid extension on postpartum contraception use in South Carolina.
journal: American journal of obstetrics & gynecology MFM
study_design: Retrospective cohort study using interrupted time series analysis.
data_years: January 1, 2019–May 31, 2021. Prepolicy period: January 1, 2019–February 28, 2020. Postpolicy period: April 1, 2020–May 31, 2021.
data_source: Linked South Carolina Medicaid claims and birth certificate data.
state_or_scope: South Carolina.
population: Medicaid-covered postpartum individuals in South Carolina. Overall sample included 54,314 individuals; analytic table includes 52,345 individuals by Medicaid coverage category.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous enrollment provision, functioning as a temporary 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension for traditional Medicaid beneficiaries. Emergency Medicaid served as a comparison group because it was exposed to the pandemic but not to the FFCRA continuous coverage protection.
outcomes: Postpartum contraception receipt within 0–2 months and 2–12 months after delivery; postpartum visit attendance.
equity_focus: Traditional Medicaid versus Emergency Medicaid coverage. This is especially relevant to immigrant-related coverage exclusions because Emergency Medicaid beneficiaries did not receive the same postpartum coverage extension
main_findings: After the FFCRA-related postpartum Medicaid extension in South Carolina, contraception use beyond 60 days postpartum increased slightly but significantly among traditional Medicaid beneficiaries. The interrupted time series estimate showed a 1.07 percentage-point monthly increase in contraception receipt from 2–12 months postpartum among traditional Medicaid beneficiaries. There was no significant change among Emergency Medicaid beneficiaries. Postpartum visit attendance decreased slightly but not significantly.
limitations: Single-state study, so findings may not generalize to other Medicaid policy environments. The FFCRA policy occurred during the COVID-19 pandemic, making it difficult to fully separate policy effects from pandemic effects. The study could not capture contraception obtained outside the Medicaid billing system or postpartum visits outside the standard 6-week visit window.
notes: Include as service_specific_medicaid_access_policy if you are keeping the contraception articles together. It is also post-2021 implementation evidence, but the main outcome is service-specific postpartum contraception access under Medicaid continuous coverage.

title: Implementing best practices for the provision of long-acting reversible contraception: a survey of obstetrician-gynecologists.
abstract: To examine obstetrician-gynecologists' practices regarding provision of long-acting reversible contraceptive (LARC) methods same-day, immediately postpartum, or to women under age 21.; Between August 2016 and March 2017, the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) sent 2500 of their members an electronic survey questionnaire regarding the provision of LARC methods. ACOG mailed nonresponders paper surveys.; After exclusions, the final sample was 1280 respondents (52.2% response rate). Although 91% of obstetrician-gynecologists reported providing IUDs, only 29% (95% CI, 26-32%) offered same-day placement. Ninety-two percent (95% CI, 90-94%) offered IUDs to eligible patients under age 21. Nineteen percent (95% CI, 16.1-21.3%) offered immediate postpartum IUD placement and 21% (95% CI, 18-23%) offered immediate postpartum implant placement. Obstetrician-gynecologists practicing in states where Medicaid reimbursed for immediate postpartum LARC devices within the global fee for delivery (versus separate reimbursement) had lower odds of offering them.; While most ob-gyns are offering IUDs to women under age 21, many are still not offering them same-day. A minority of ob-gyns offer either IUDs or implants immediately postpartum, and there are important geographic and practice setting disparities in this practice.; Efforts to align LARC practices with published evidence and improve access to LARC methods for women desiring them will require a multipronged effort including continuing education of physicians, patient education and outreach, as well as advocacy to improve insurance coverage and reimbursement.
study_design: Cross-sectional survey study of obstetrician-gynecologists.
data_years: Survey fielded August 2016–March 2017.
data_source: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists survey of ACOG Fellows and Junior Fellows.
state_or_scope: United States; national sample of practicing OB-GYNs.
population: Practicing obstetrician-gynecologists. Final analytic sample included 1,149 respondents after exclusions
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Medicaid reimbursement policy for immediate postpartum LARC, especially whether Medicaid reimbursed postpartum LARC devices separately from the global delivery fee versus within the global fee.
outcomes: Provision of IUDs, same-day IUD placement, IUD provision to patients under age 21, immediate postpartum IUD placement, immediate postpartum implant placement, and association with Medicaid reimbursement policy.
equity_focus: Geographic and practice-setting variation in access. The study also addresses Medicaid reimbursement as a structural access barrier for postpartum contraception.
main_findings: Most OB-GYNs offered IUDs, but only 28.5% offered same-day placement. Only 18.6% offered immediate postpartum IUD placement and 20.5% offered immediate postpartum implant placement. OB-GYNs in states where Medicaid reimbursed immediate postpartum LARC devices within the global delivery fee had lower odds of offering immediate postpartum LARC than those in states with separate reimbursement.
limitations: Self-reported physician survey, possible nonresponse and desirability bias. The study does not measure patient-level LARC receipt or outcomes. It also does not fully assess logistical barriers such as prior authorization, clinic workflow, device stocking, or on-site laboratory services.
notes: Include as service_specific_medicaid_access_policy. This is not about postpartum Medicaid coverage extension, but it is empirical and directly relevant to Medicaid reimbursement policy as a postpartum service-access mechanism

title: Postpartum Medicaid Coverage Expansion and Changes in the Risk of Health Insurance Loss Within the Second Year After Birth.
abstract: To determine whether pandemic-era Medicaid policies to increase postpartum coverage to 1 year were effective in preventing coverage loss into the second-year postpartum. The analytic sample included 7967 cases (N = 4632 in the pandemic era) from the 2019 and 2021-2024 Current Population Survey, Annual Social and Economic Supplement. On multivariable analysis of the entire sample, era was not associated with the type or continuity of insurance coverage. Among families living below 100% Federal Poverty Level, the relative risk of coverage gaps compared with continuous private coverage decreased by 58% (95% confidence interval: 19%, 79%, P = 0.010). Pandemic-era Medicaid policies appeared effective in preventing postpartum coverage loss in the second year after birth, especially among families living below the poverty line.
study_design: Population-based repeated cross-sectional study using CPS-ASEC data and multinomial logistic regression.
data_years: 2019 and 2021–2024 CPS-ASEC data; 2019 = prepandemic period, 2021–2024 = pandemic/post-extension era. 2020 excluded.
data_source: Current Population Survey, Annual Social and Economic Supplement.
state_or_scope: United States, nationally representative survey sample.
population: Female respondents aged 18–44 living with a child at least 1 year old but younger than 2 years old; analytic sample N = 7,976.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Pandemic-era postpartum Medicaid coverage policies, including FFCRA continuous Medicaid enrollment and ARPA 12-month postpartum Medicaid extension, which expanded/maintained Medicaid coverage through the first postpartum year.
outcomes: Insurance coverage during the second year after birth: continuous private coverage, continuous public coverage, continuous uninsurance, and part-year coverage gaps.
equity_focus: Income-based subgroup analysis for families below 100% FPL; also adjusts for race/ethnicity, citizenship, marital status, employment, Medicaid expansion state, and household income.
main_findings: Overall, pandemic-era policy was not associated with major changes in insurance type or continuity in the second year after birth. Among families below 100% FPL, the relative risk of coverage gaps compared with continuous private coverage decreased by about 58%, suggesting that first-year postpartum Medicaid extensions did not simply delay coverage loss into the second postpartum year for the lowest-income families
limitations: CPS-ASEC does not report insurance coverage at delivery, so Medicaid eligibility at birth is inferred indirectly. The second postpartum year is approximated using the child’s age, and the 12-month lookback may include some first-year postpartum coverage experiences. Insurance is self-reported and may be misclassified. The study cannot identify biological children in the household or chronic health conditions.
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. This article is useful because it asks whether 12-month postpartum Medicaid coverage prevents or merely delays insurance loss beyond the first postpartum year.

title: "Where There's a Will There's a Way": The Impact of State Variations in the Implementation of Continuous Coverage on Access to Postpartum Care During the Pandemic Emergency.
abstract: To explore how the Medicaid continuous coverage requirement and unwinding process was implemented in three states with diverse existing policy environments and implications for the implementation of post-emergency 12-month postpartum extensions.; State data on unwinding performance and qualitative in-depth interviews with 48 stakeholders and patient-facing healthcare workers in Texas, New York and New Jersey.; State Medicaid stakeholders and patient-facing healthcare workers in each state were interviewed with the goal of gaining insights into: (1) How the continuous coverage requirement was implemented; (2) What effects continuous coverage had on access to care for postpartum mothers; (3) How states are implementing the pandemic unwinding and postpartum extensions.; Interviews were recorded and took place over Zoom between September 2022 and March 2024. Interview transcripts were analyzed thematically using an open coding approach.; The study found that low awareness of the continuous coverage requirement blunted potential benefits: little changed in practice in recommended patient care or follow-up procedures. Differences in state unwinding approaches reflected differential state capacity to minimize procedural disenrollment and political incentives to either rapidly reduce or maintain Medicaid rolls. Based on these findings, we describe how political will, state capacity and policy legacies interact to either increase or decrease administrative burdens associated with program enrollment/re-enrollment.; While the continuous coverage requirement in theory applies equally across US states, its implementation was inconsistent and influenced by state capacity and political objectives creating differential experiences across states. To ensure that postpartum Medicaid coverage extensions have maximal impact on improving maternal health, states should develop improved communication strategies to ensure that providers and community-based organizations are aware of coverage changes and leverage available flexibilities to increase use of administrative renewal and ensure smooth coverage transitions.
study_design: Qualitative comparative case study with state unwinding performance data and in-depth interviews.
data_years: Qualitative comparative case study with state unwinding performance data and in-depth interviews.
data_source: 48 qualitative interviews with Medicaid stakeholders and patient-facing health workers; state unwinding performance data from KFF tracker and state Medicaid caseload data
state_or_scope: Texas, New York, and New Jersey.
population: State Medicaid stakeholders, policy/advocacy participants, government actors, enrollment/billing specialists, community health workers, social workers, doulas/midwives, nurse practitioners, and OB-GYNs involved in Medicaid coverage and maternal health policy/program implementation.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous coverage requirement, Medicaid unwinding, and transition to 12-month postpartum Medicaid extensions. The study focuses on how state implementation, administrative burden, communication, ex-parte renewal, and political/state capacity shaped postpartum coverage access
outcomes: Implementation of continuous coverage; communication and awareness of coverage changes; state unwinding approaches; procedural disenrollment; ex-parte renewal; state capacity and political will; implications for postpartum care access.
equity_focus: State variation, immigrant eligibility, administrative burden, and differential implementation across Texas, New York, and New Jersey. The paper specifically compares state treatment of immigrant coverage and postpartum extension inclusion.
main_findings: mplementation varied substantially by state despite the federal continuous coverage requirement. Low awareness among providers and patients blunted potential benefits for postpartum care. New York had stronger systems and political will to maintain coverage, New Jersey had supportive goals but weaker implementation capacity, and Texas had higher procedural disenrollment and more burdensome unwinding. The authors argue that state capacity, political will, and policy legacies shaped whether administrative burdens increased or decreased during unwinding and postpartum extension implementation
limitations: Findings are based on qualitative interviews and stakeholder/provider perspectives, which may be partial. The study covers only three states and may not generalize nationally. The authors had limited access to managed care organizations and Texas state workers.
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. This is especially useful for explaining why coverage extensions alone may not translate into access without communication, administrative capacity, and low-burden renewal processes

title: Effects of the continuous Medicaid coverage provision of the family first coronavirus response act on postpartum Medicaid coverage, depression symptoms, and birth control use.
abstract: The 2020 Family First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) extended Medicaid coverage for enrollees without rechecking eligibility. Before that, women were eligible for Medicaid coverage up to 60 days postpartum. We examine the FFCRA's effect on Medicaid postpartum coverage, depression symptoms, and birth control use beyond 60 days after delivery.; We use data from the 2016-2021 Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System (PRAMS). The primary sample includes 56,828 women who were income eligible up to 60 days postpartum or beyond in their state.; We employ a difference-in-differences design comparing outcomes before and after the FFCRA between women who were not income eligible for Medicaid coverage beyond 60 days postpartum in their state and those who were income eligible. We estimate models without and with controlling for state-specific trends in outcomes over time.; N/A.; There is an 8.1 percentage-point increase in the likelihood of having Medicaid coverage without controlling for state-specific trends, and 5.4 percentage-points when controlling for state-specific trends (both p < 0.05). There is a decline in likelihood of being uninsured by 3 percentage-points (p < 0.05) without state-specific trends and a smaller and non-significant decline when including state-specific trends. Estimated effects on depression symptoms and birth control use are small and statistically non-significant.; Following the FFCRA, there was an increase in Medicaid coverage beyond 60 days postpartum among women who would have been income ineligible for Medicaid after 60 days. However, there is a less pronounced effect on likelihood of being uninsured, which might be partly due to some switching from private to Medicaid coverage. There were no discernable effects on depression symptoms and birth control use. Examining additional health and health care utilization measures over a longer postpartum period is needed to further understand the FCCRA effects on these outcomes.
study_design: Difference-in-differences study using PRAMS survey data. Treatment/control status was assigned at the individual level based on whether a woman would have been income-ineligible for Medicaid after 60 days postpartum under her state’s nonpregnancy/parent eligibility rules.
data_years: 2016–2021 PRAMS data.
data_source: Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System linked with state Medicaid eligibility thresholds from KFF.
state_or_scope: Primary analysis included 22 states with PRAMS data during FFCRA years and adequate pre-FFCRA data; sensitivity analysis used 14 states with data in every year from 2016–2021.
population: Women who met income eligibility criteria for Medicaid during pregnancy in their state. Primary sample included 56,828 women.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA continuous Medicaid coverage provision, which prevented states from redetermining eligibility and disenrolling Medicaid beneficiaries, effectively extending postpartum Medicaid coverage beyond the usual 60-day postpartum limit
outcomes: Postpartum Medicaid coverage, private coverage, uninsurance, postpartum depression symptoms, birth control use, and postpartum checkup completion.
equity_focus: Heterogeneity analyses by race/ethnicity, preconception health conditions, Medicaid expansion status, and size of the pregnancy-versus-nonpregnancy eligibility gap.
main_findings: FFCRA increased postpartum Medicaid coverage by 8.1 percentage points without state-specific trend controls and by 5.4 percentage points with state-specific trend controls. Uninsurance declined by about 3 percentage points without state-specific trends, but the decline became smaller and nonsignificant after adding state-specific trends. There were no significant effects on postpartum depression symptoms or birth control use
limitations: Outcomes were self-reported, so Medicaid coverage may be underreported, especially if beneficiaries were unaware of continued eligibility. PRAMS captures outcomes mostly around 3–6 months postpartum, limiting ability to assess later postpartum effects. PRAMS has limited measures of care utilization and maternal health. Income may change between pregnancy and postpartum, which could affect eligibility classification
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. This is useful because it uses a different DiD approach than Daw et al. and reinforces that FFCRA increased postpartum Medicaid coverage, while showing limited short-term effects on depression symptoms and birth control use

title: The Affordable Care Act and Changes in Women's Health Insurance Coverage Before, During, and After Pregnancy in California.
abstract: Having health insurance is associated with improvements in health care access and use, health behaviors, and outcomes. We examined changes in health insurance coverage for California women before, during, and after pregnancy after implementation of the Affordable Care Act (ACA).; We used data from the 2011-2017 California Maternal and Infant Health Assessment, an annual representative survey of women sampled from birth certificates (n = 47 487). We examined health insurance coverage at baseline before ACA implementation (2011-2013) and in each survey year from 2014 to 2017 for 3 periods (before, during, and after pregnancy). We calculated prevalence ratios to evaluate changes in health insurance coverage, adjusting for changes in demographic characteristics. Few women were uninsured during pregnancy before implementation of the ACA; therefore, analyses focused on health insurance before pregnancy and postpartum.; Before ACA implementation, 24.4% of women reported being uninsured before pregnancy, which decreased to 10.1% in 2017. About 17% of women reported being uninsured postpartum before ACA implementation, and this percentage decreased to 7.5% in 2017. ACA implementation resulted in a >50% adjusted decline in the likelihood of being uninsured before pregnancy or postpartum, primarily because of substantial increases in Medicaid coverage.; ACA implementation resulted in a dramatic reduction in mothers in California who were uninsured before and after pregnancy. Medicaid expansion played a major role in this improvement.
study_design: Repeated cross-sectional survey study using California MIHA data.
data_source: California Maternal and Infant Health Assessment survey linked to birth certificate data.
state_or_scope: California
population: Women in California with a recent live birth; analytic sample included 47,487 respondents.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: Affordable Care Act implementation and California Medi-Cal expansion, including expanded eligibility for low-income adults and childless adults. The study examines how ACA/Medi-Cal expansion affected insurance coverage before pregnancy, during pregnancy, and postpartum
outcomes: Insurance coverage before pregnancy, during pregnancy, and postpartum; Medi-Cal coverage; private insurance; uninsured status.
equity_focus: Race/ethnicity/nativity, language, income, education, marital status, and parity. The study highlights persistent postpartum uninsurance among non–US-born Latinx women despite overall gains.
main_findings: After ACA implementation, the percentage uninsured before pregnancy declined from 24.4% at baseline to 10.1% in 2017. Postpartum uninsurance declined from 17.4% to 7.5%. Adjusted models showed more than a 50% decline in the likelihood of being uninsured before pregnancy and postpartum, largely driven by increases in Medi-Cal coverage. However, non–US-born Latinx women still had high postpartum uninsurance in 2017 at 24.1%
limitations: Single-state study, so findings may not generalize nationally. Insurance coverage was self-reported. The study does not have a comparison state, so findings are associational rather than causal. Immigration status was not directly measured.
notes: Include as pre-2021 baseline problem evidence. It is not a 12-month postpartum extension study, but it provides strong empirical evidence that Medicaid expansion reduced postpartum uninsurance while leaving persistent coverage gaps, especially for immigrant/Latinx populations.

title: Racial Inequities in Postpartum Coverage During Medicaid Continuous Coverage: Evidence From Affordable Care Act Expansion Versus Nonexpansion States.
abstract: Extending pregnancy Medicaid coverage to 12 months after delivery is a key strategy to reduce maternal morbidity and mortality and may be most impactful among states that did not expand Medicaid as part of the Affordable Care Act. The continuous Medicaid coverage provision enacted at the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic provides a natural experiment to test the impact of postpartum Medicaid extension policies.; A cross-sectional pre-post design was used to estimate changes in postpartum insurance after the introduction of continuous Medicaid coverage. Using 2016-2023 American Community Survey data, non-Hispanic Black and non-Hispanic White respondents aged 19-50 years who gave birth in the previous 12 months were included (N=157,016). Postpartum insurance was categorized as Medicaid, private, or uninsured. Linear regression models compared expansion with nonexpansion states within Black and White respondents for each year before and after continuous coverage, with 2019 as the reference year. Covariate-adjusted regressions included respondents' age, employment status, and household income.; Prepandemic uninsurance rates among Black women were 16.5% in nonexpansion states and 6.4% in expansion states, and among White women, they were 11.1% and 5.4%, respectively. Among Black women, uninsurance decreased after continuous coverage in nonexpansion states only. For example, in 2023, Black postpartum uninsurance decreased in nonexpansion states by 5.2 percentage points more (95% CI= -8.9, -1.5) than in expansion states; this was driven by a 3.9 percentage points larger gain in Medicaid in nonexpansion states than in expansion states (95% CI= -1.8, 9.6). Among White women, uninsurance decreases in 2021-2023 were also larger in nonexpansion states but were driven by gains in private insurance. As a result, Black-White disparities in postpartum uninsurance remained unchanged.; Postpartum uninsurance decreased among Black women in nonexpansion states after continuous coverage, but racial inequities persisted.
study_design: Cross-sectional pre-post study using American Community Survey data and linear regression models.
data_years: 2016–2023 ACS data; 2020 excluded from the main design; 2019 used as the reference year.
data_source: American Community Survey
state_or_scope: United States; comparison of ACA Medicaid expansion versus nonexpansion states.
population: Non-Hispanic Black and non-Hispanic White respondents aged 19–50 who gave birth in the previous 12 months; N = 157,016.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous coverage provision, which functioned as a de facto postpartum Medicaid extension. The study evaluates whether coverage changes differed by state ACA Medicaid expansion status.
outcomes: Postpartum insurance status: Medicaid, private insurance, and uninsurance.
equity_focus: Race and Medicaid expansion status. The study specifically examines Black–White inequities in postpartum uninsurance and whether continuous coverage reduced inequities differently in expansion versus nonexpansion states.
main_findings: Before the pandemic, postpartum uninsurance was highest in nonexpansion states, especially among Black women. After continuous coverage, uninsurance decreased among Black women in nonexpansion states, driven by Medicaid gains. Among White women, uninsurance also decreased in nonexpansion states, but more through private coverage gains. Because both groups improved, Black–White inequities in postpartum uninsurance persisted.
limitations: Because continuous coverage applied nationally, the study used a pre-post design without an untreated comparison group. ACS data cannot identify the exact month postpartum when coverage occurred. Insurance coverage was self-reported and may undercount Medicaid coverage, especially if respondents were unaware of continued eligibility. 2023 was also a transition year between continuous coverage unwinding and state postpartum extensions.
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation evidence. Strong equity-focused article because it shows that postpartum Medicaid extension improved coverage in nonexpansion states but did not eliminate racial inequities.

title: Postpartum Insurance Rates Among Citizens Versus Noncitizens During the COVID-19 Public Health Emergency.
abstract: To estimate the effect of Medicaid continuous coverage provision (CCP) during the COVID-19 public health emergency on postpartum insurance for low-income noncitizen immigrants compared to citizens, and to assess whether state use of the from-conception-to-the-end-of-pregnancy (FCEP) policy, which excluded many immigrants from postpartum coverage, modified this effect.; This study used a quasi-experimental design (difference-in-differences, or DID) to compare the insurance coverage gains between low-income postpartum noncitizens and citizens, using 2019 as the reference year. We further applied a triple difference (DDD) approach to assess the effect of the FCEP-only coverage policy, hypothesizing that noncitizens in states using FCEP as the sole pathway would experience smaller gains than in other states using various pathways to cover pregnant noncitizens otherwise ineligible.; Using the 2016-2022 American Community Survey individual-level data, we identified 61,572 low-income citizens (weighted N = 7,553,823) and 10,444 low-income noncitizens (weighted N = 1,487,373), with 8 FCEP-only states and 23 other states with various pathways.; During the study period, the average postpartum uninsurance rate was 44.6% for noncitizens and 14.0% for citizens, while Medicaid coverage was 34.5% for noncitizens and 57.5% for citizens. Medicaid gains among noncitizens were statistically similar to gains among citizens in 2021 compared to 2019 in both FCEP-only states (DID = 2.3 percentage points [pp], 95% CI: -13.3, 8.7) and states with various pathways (DID = 4.6 pp, 95% CI: -1.2, 10.4), but these trends did not persist in 2022. We found no differences in insurance coverage gains among noncitizens between FCEP-only and states with various pathways.; Findings suggest that the CCP did not substantially reduce postpartum coverage inequities for noncitizen immigrants in either FCEP-only states or states with various pathways. Postpartum coverage inequities persisted under the CCPs, leaving many noncitizens uninsured.
study_design: Quasi-experimental study using difference-in-differences and triple-difference models.
data_years: 2016–2022 ACS data; 2019 used as the reference year; 2021 and 2022 evaluated as post-continuous coverage years.
data_source: American Community Survey individual-level microdata from IPUMS USA.
state_or_scope: Multistate U.S. analysis. Main comparison included 8 FCEP-only states and 23 states with other or multiple pathways for covering pregnant noncitizens.
population: Low-income postpartum women under 200% FPL who gave birth in the past 12 months; 61,572 citizens and 10,444 noncitizens.
medicaid_policy_mechanism: FFCRA Medicaid continuous coverage provision during the COVID-19 public health emergency, with attention to whether states’ use of FCEP-only coverage limited postpartum coverage gains for noncitizens.
outcomes: Postpartum uninsurance, Medicaid coverage, and private insurance coverage.
equity_focus: Citizenship status, immigrant coverage exclusions, Hispanic noncitizens, and state policy pathways for noncitizen pregnancy/postpartum coverage.
main_findings: Postpartum coverage inequities between citizens and noncitizens persisted. Across the study period, noncitizens had much higher uninsurance than citizens, 44.6% versus 14.0%, and lower Medicaid coverage, 34.5% versus 57.5%. Medicaid gains among noncitizens were not statistically different from gains among citizens in 2021, and gains did not persist in 2022. The study found no clear evidence that FCEP-only states differed significantly from states with various pathways in reducing citizen–noncitizen coverage inequities
limitations: ACS does not identify exact legal immigration status, so the study cannot separate undocumented immigrants, recent lawful permanent residents, and noncitizens otherwise eligible for Medicaid. Noncitizen sample sizes were small, reducing statistical power. Insurance coverage was self-reported and may undercount Medicaid coverage. The study also cannot fully distinguish legal ineligibility from enrollment barriers such as language access, outreach, fear, or administrative burden
notes: Include as post-2021 policy/implementation and equity evidence. This is important because it shows that FFCRA continuous coverage did not substantially close postpartum coverage gaps for noncitizens, even though some coverage gains occurred. It strengthens the review’s equity argument around immigrant exclusions and implementation limits
